data: Four lines of Greek text, with a Latin inscription on the reverse.
Dated by Manganaro to 4th century CE, Ferrua suggests could be specifically 417 CE
Found in the suburb of S.Lucia, Siracusa (acquired by the museum in 1925_
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana

An outstanding collection of archaeological material. The collection ranges from Siracusa and the surrounding territory (Megara Hyblaia, Akrai, Kasmenai etc) to encompass all of eastern Sicily, reaching as far as Gela and Agrigento. The ground floor covers prehistory through to the end of the Classical period. Upstairs includes Hellenistic and Roman Syracuse and a display of material from the catacombs. A gallery displaying material from Hellenistic eastern Sicily is in preparation.
Association pour l’encouragement des études greques, « Bulletin épigraphique », Revue des études grecques, 1888, http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/797735566
Antonio Ferrua, «Florilegio d’iscrizioni paleocristiane di Sicilia», Rendiconti della pontificia Accademia Romana di Archeologia 22 (1947 1946): 227–39
Le iscrizioni datate della Sicilia paleocristiana
Antonio Ferrua, «Le iscrizioni datate della Sicilia paleocristiana», Kokalos 28–29 (1983 1982): 3–29
Greco nei pagi e latino nelle città della Sicilia romana tra I e VI sec. d.C.
Giacomo Manganaro, «Greco nei pagi e latino nelle città della Sicilia romana tra I e VI sec. d.C.», in l’epigrafia del villaggio, a c. di A. Calbi, A. Donati, e G. Poma (Faenza, 1993), 543–94

Ferrua, 1946-1947, Florilegio d'iscrizioni paleocristiane di Sicilia

ΡΟΥΦΙΝΑΧΑΙΡΟΥΣΑΕΝΧΡΙΣΤΩΕΤΗΞΕΑΝΕΠΑΥΣΑΤΟΠΡΟΙΔΚΑΛΑΝΔΩΝΙΑΝΟΥΑΡΙΩΝΕΠΙΤΗΙΑΩΡΑ

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text of Ferrua 1946-47 ;
  • line.4: Ferrua 1982-83: no.44 alternatively: ἐπ(ὶ τῇ) ια (ὑπατίᾳ); Feissel 1987: ἐπι(νεμήσεως) αʹ or ἐπι(νεμήσεως) ιαʹ

English translation

Jonathan Prag

Rouphina, rejoicing in Christ, died at 65 years of age, 14 days before the Kalends of January (19 December), at the 11th hour [or: in the 11th consulship (of Honorius); or in the 1st/11th indiction]

commentary

The reverse of the stone was re-used for a later Latin inscription (ISic001669).
